summary: Carbondale's affordable-housing pipeline at the donated Town Center Property transitioned from an Artspace LIHTC partnership (denied in CHFA 2024 R1) to a fresh master-developer RFP. Funding stack: a voter-approved 6% short-term-rental excise tax dedicated to housing, a ~$1M housing fund built through annual budget transfers, the 2023 Community Housing Plan, and a $100K commitment to the regional West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (Good Deeds buy-down program). Carbondale's deed-restricted Community Housing Program is administered under contract by the Garfield County Housing Authority.

paragraphs.text: On December 28, 2021, the Carbondale Board of Trustees voted at a special meeting to accept an anonymous gift of 14 vacant parcels in the Town Center development — roughly 1.4 acres directly across Colorado Avenue from Carbondale Town Hall and the recreation center, near Thunder River Theatre. The county assessor valued the combined parcels at close to $2.7 million; the grantor retained a private parking easement.
The town later established the Town Center Connect engagement page to socialize concepts for a community-led affordable / creative-housing development on the donated parcels.

After a competitive RFP that drew eight responses (narrowed to five finalists), the Board of Trustees selected national arts-focused nonprofit developer Artspace Projects, Inc. as the original master developer; Artspace partnered with Colorado's Space to Create program.

The Board approved Phase 1 schematic design in early 2024 — a 39-unit program (6 studios, 10 1-BR, 19 2-BR, 4 3-BR) plus approximately 2,000 sq ft of commercial space at 80-150% AMI — to support a competitive 9% LIHTC application to CHFA Round One 2024.
CHFA's May 30, 2024 Round One award announcement lists 13 awarded projects across Colorado; the Carbondale Town Center proposal is absent from the awardee list, confirming the application was denied.

Per Andrew Michaelson of Artspace, reported by the Sopris Sun in July 2024, the project team returned with CHFA's feedback on the denial and prepared to re-apply in February 2025; the partnership at that point intended to refine the budget and re-submit rather than abandon the site.

Artspace's contract on the Town Center project concluded in late 2024 / early 2025. The Town subsequently engaged Robert Schultz Consulting to draft a fresh Request for Proposals to secure a new master developer for the parcels; the community-guided concept work and architectural schematics developed under the Artspace partnership are available to the next team.

Carbondale voters passed Ballot Issue 2A on November 8, 2022, enacting a 6% short-term-rental excise tax that took effect January 1, 2023. The ballot language and the town's own Finance Department FAQ confirm the revenue is dedicated primarily to fund 'the promotion, regulation, development and protection of affordable and attainable housing, projects and programs, including capital expenditures' — a recurring revenue stream that compounds one-time capital pledges.

As of November 2023, annual budget transfers and conservative spending had built the Town's housing fund to approximately $1 million, explicitly available to advance the Town Center development and other housing goals. The 2024 adopted municipal budget formalizes the recurring transfers from the STR tax fund and the general fund that back these commitments.

The Town adopted its first Community Housing Plan in 2023, organizing the Town Center development, the Town's deed-restricted Community Housing Program, and participation in regional initiatives under a single policy framework. On November 12, 2024 the Board approved Ordinance No. 10, Series of 2024, amending the Town's inclusionary residential-housing requirements.

Carbondale contracts with the Garfield County Housing Authority to administer qualifications, sales, improvement approvals, and other compliance aspects of the Town's deed-restricted Community Housing Program — the regional housing authority acts as the back-office for the Town's local inclusionary stock.

In April 2024 the Board approved a separate Letter of Intent committing $500,000 toward acquisition of the Cavern Springs Mobile Home Park to preserve its existing affordable units; Mayor Ben Bohmfalk publicly described $500,000-$600,000 as the town's comfort level for housing-preservation pledges of this scale. The commitment was later expanded to roughly $1 million as the town pushed neighboring jurisdictions to match it.

Carbondale is a member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit incorporated in 2020 and fully organized in 2023 to operate a regional workforce-housing pipeline across the Aspen-to-Parachute commuter corridor spanning Garfield, Pitkin, and Eagle counties. The coalition's flagship program is branded 'Good Deeds Housing.' This section describes coalition-level activity in which Carbondale formally participates; coalition outcomes are not Carbondale-specific deliverables.

Via Resolution 9-2023, the Carbondale Board of Trustees committed up to $100,000 to WMRHC to support administration of the Good Deeds buy-down program in Carbondale. Aspen Public Radio reporting documents Carbondale's $100K alongside larger pilot commitments from Pitkin County ($1M), Aspen ($450K), Snowmass Village ($250K), and Glenwood Springs ($200K).
The coalition's stated 2026 goal is to convert 30 free-market homes in the Roaring Fork Valley and adjacent corridor to deed-restricted housing, at an estimated total program cost of $8M-$10M.

WMRHC's Good Deeds program is a flat ~30% buy-down contribution toward the purchase price of a qualifying home (up to a $1.5M home price), recorded against a permanent price-capped deed restriction with appreciation capped at approximately 3% per year. (Note: the 5%-Resident-Occupied / 15%-Price-Capped buy-down structure is the adjacent Housing Eagle County Good Deeds program, not WMRHC's.) Carbondale-area buy-down price points have been quoted around $1.2 million.

Because LIHTC awards are site-specific and tied to precise local land parcels, the coalition's pooled-capital allocation does not include LIHTC applications. The coalition functions as a workforce-housing safety net and a unified local matching source that can strengthen a member jurisdiction's LIHTC pro-forma by demonstrating committed gap-funding capacity.
